L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes strong, sustained winds with horizontal flags that were observable at the exit point. Two companions decided conditions were too dangerous and walked down. The jumper was warned but chose to jump anyway, driven by time pressure ('desperate to get one final jump in before his flight home'). The wind conditions were clearly identifiable as unsafe before the jump, making the decision to jump a human error rather than an unforeseeable environmental event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump in strong, sustained wind conditions that multiple companions judged too dangerous, with flags at a nearby restaurant flying horizontal. Two partners declined to jump and explicitly warned him against it. The wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration capability, pushing him back into the cliff wall, directly producing the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and reluctance to waste the trip created misplaced motivation to proceed despite clearly dangerous conditions, directly contributing to his decision to jump when two partners walked down and explicitly warned him.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite observable evidence that conditions were too dangerous (horizontal flags, sustained gusts, 45 minutes of worsening conditions, two experienced partners declining), the jumper believed he could safely complete the jump. His dismissal of explicit warnings ('he didn't care') indicates he overestimated his ability or his canopy's capability to handle the wind conditions.

L0_description: The bridle was misrouted around the right leg strap during packing or gearing up, preventing canopy extraction and causing a total malfunction (PC in tow). The narrative explicitly states the jumper packed in a rush and that neither the jumper nor his partner performed a gear check, which would have immediately caught the misrouting. This constitutes human error in rigging/assembly and failure to perform pre-jump inspection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to perform a gear check before exiting, an action he knew to be necessary and would have performed if reminded. The narrative explicitly states that neither the jumper nor his partner traced the bridle routing, and that a simple hand-check along the bridle would have immediately caught the misrouted bridle locked against the leg strap. This omission allowed the fatal rigging error to go undetected.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper misrouted the bridle around the right leg strap during packing, preventing the pilot chute from extracting the canopy from the container. This incorrect bridle routing resulted in a total malfunction (PC in tow with container remaining closed) and fatal ground impact. The packing was performed in a rush the evening before the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rush the evening before because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch early morning conditions. This haste during packing contributed to the technique error of misrouting the bridle around the leg strap, a rigging mistake that would likely have been caught with careful, unhurried packing.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a gear check at the exit point despite standing right next to each other. The narrative describes this as a straightforward omission rather than time pressure or distraction at the exit, suggesting a failure to maintain sufficient alertness to standard pre-jump procedures. This inattention enabled the memory failure of skipping the gear check that would have caught the misrouted bridle.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's immediate pre-jump preparation was inadequate: he did not trace the bridle routing before exiting, nor did his partner. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle would have immediately detected the misrouting. This preparatory failure directly enabled the memory-lapse unsafe act by allowing the packing error to persist undetected to the point of exit.

L0_description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line in flat-light conditions (snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) that eliminated depth perception cues, leading to fatal loss of altitude awareness and ground impact without deployment initiation. Flat light over snow is an observable and forecastable environmental condition that a competent BASE jumper could assess before exiting; the decision to jump in these conditions constitutes a human error in judgment rather than an unforeseeable environmental event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Visual illusion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Flat light conditions (heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture on the snowfield) eliminated all depth cues, causing the jumper to misperceive his altitude above the terrain. He maintained a relaxed body posture with no urgency and no deployment initiation, clearly believing he had plenty of altitude. He flew into the snow at full speed with zero pilot input suggesting he perceived the ground's proximity. The visual environment caused him to perceive himself as much higher than he actually was, resulting in no deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture created complete flat light conditions that eliminated all visual depth cues. The jumper's sensory systems failed to correctly perceive his altitude above the snowfield, causing him to believe he had plenty of altitude when he was at ground level. This perceptual degradation directly produced the Layer 1 visual illusion / perceptual error.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for flat light
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line over a featureless snowfield on a day with heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, no shadows, and no contrast. Adequate pre-jump site assessment should have identified that these flat light conditions would eliminate the depth cues necessary for altitude awareness during proximity flight over snow terrain. This preparation failure created the conditions under which the perceptual error occurred.

L0_description: The narrative provides positive evidence that the fatality resulted from a manufacturing defect in the harness webbing (incorrect heat treatment during production causing internal degradation undetectable through normal inspection). The jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, equipment selection, and execution were all confirmed correct. The equipment failure was independent of any human error by the jumper, constituting an unforeseeable material failure.

The narrative provides positive evidence of an unforeseeable environmental event: a massive localized rotor caused by a temperature inversion and sudden thermal event that was confirmed by meteorologists to be undetectable and unpredictable from the exit point. The jumper's exit, tracking, deployment, and opening were all described as correct. Conditions appeared dead calm with no wind indicators showing movement. The environmental factor was not reasonably identifiable before or during the jump.

The fatality resulted from a massive brainstem stroke caused by a congenital arterial malformation that had never produced symptoms and was undetected on a recent physical. The jumper had no prior medical history or foreseeable risk factors. Equipment was recovered in perfect condition, and the jump was conducted in perfect conditions with proper preparation, placing this outside the jumper's reasonable anticipation or control.

The narrative explicitly identifies two human errors: the jumper chose to exit in wind conditions (20 knots sustained, gusts over 30) that four experienced companions had assessed as unsafe and stood down from, and he then executed an unstable exit without prioritizing deployment. The wind conditions were observable and forecastable—the group had been monitoring them all morning—making this a decision error rather than an unforeseeable environmental event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in sustained 20-knot crosswinds with gusts exceeding 30 knots—conditions that four experienced companions had unanimously deemed unsafe and stood down from. This decision to launch into known dangerous wind conditions directly produced the fatal sequence by exposing him to a crosswind gust that pushed him into the wall. The wind conditions were fully observable before the jump and warranted an abort.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the crosswind gust destabilized him on exit, the jumper went head-low within the first second and tumbled along the cliff face for three to four seconds without ever getting a hand to his pilot chute. He failed to prioritize deploying the parachute over recovering stability, contrary to accepted practice which dictates that in a poor exit position the first priority is to deploy regardless of body position. This failure to deploy produced the subterminal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: After all four experienced companions unanimously stood down due to dangerous wind conditions, the jumper chose to remain at the exit and wait, then launched alone. This decision to persist after the entire group had called the conditions unsafe suggests misplaced motivation—reluctance to abandon the effort after spending the morning at the exit, or a drive to jump that overrode the clear social and environmental signals to abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust on exit
L2_preconditions.description: The strong crosswind gust that caught the jumper immediately upon exit pushed him sideways and back toward the wall, destabilizing him from stable to head-low within the first second. This environmental force directly degraded his ability to maintain a stable exit and reach his pilot chute, contributing to the technique error of failing to deploy during the ensuing tumble along the cliff face.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to this fatality. The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before (vomiting, headache, visibly unsteady), which degraded his physical coordination and contributed to an unstable exit. He then failed to deploy his pilot chute during approximately 6 seconds of freefall, fixating on stability recovery rather than prioritizing deployment—violating the fundamental principle that deploying in any position is preferable to impact with nothing out.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's exit technique failed - his legs buckled on the push-off, resulting in an immediately unstable body position with a slow tumble. The narrative explicitly attributes this to hangover-degraded coordination, but the failure itself was in physical execution of the exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Once unstable and tumbling, the jumper fixated entirely on recovering stability rather than deploying the pilot chute. He spent the entire approximately 6 seconds of freefall attempting to regain a belly-to-earth position instead of pitching the PC. The narrative explicitly states that even unstable he could have pitched and taken whatever opening he got. The decision to prioritize stability recovery over deployment at sub-terminal altitude from a cliff was the direct producing mechanism of the closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was severely hungover from heavy drinking until 4am the night before. The narrative explicitly states the hangover affected his coordination, directly contributing to his legs buckling on the exit push-off and the resulting unstable body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper fixated entirely on recovering stability during the approximately 6 seconds of freefall, to the exclusion of the critical task of deploying the pilot chute. This narrowing of attention on body position recovery prevented him from recognizing that deployment—even from an unstable position—was the priority action needed to survive.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's severely degraded physiological state from heavy alcohol consumption the night before likely impaired his cognitive processing and decision-making capacity during the brief freefall window, contributing to his failure to prioritize deployment over stability recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that the jumper had jumped hungover before without issues, indicating a pattern of jumping while physiologically impaired. This normalization of jumping in a degraded state was a sustained failure to correct a known risky behavior, which on this occasion contributed to the exit technique failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Despite being visibly unwell—splitting headache, vomiting twice on the trail, visibly unsteady—and despite peers advising him to sit out, the jumper pressed forward with the jump, stating adrenaline would compensate. His motivation to proceed was driven by factors other than a sound assessment of his readiness.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to this fatality. The jumper used a tracking suit in BASE with grossly insufficient training (only two skydives in the suit versus the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use). He chose an intermediate-to-advanced cliff requiring effective sub-terminal tracking despite having no cliff jumping experience with the suit, ignored a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, and jumped while visibly exhausted after driving 14 hours through the night—a physical condition known to degrade performance. His inability to achieve forward separation with the unfamiliar suit directly caused the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and attempted to track but was unable to achieve an effective tracking position in the suit, resulting in steep descent with insufficient forward drive. He could not get the tracking suit to produce adequate forward separation despite a decent exit push. This inability to fly the suit at the required performance level resulted in impact with a ledge at approximately 7 seconds after exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to use a tracking suit on a slider-up cliff requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking despite having only two skydives in the suit and zero cliff jumps with it. Accepted practice requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and mastery of the suit's body position and consistent on-heading openings. This decision to use unfamiliar equipment on a demanding object directly set up the conditions for the fatal inability to achieve separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and zero cliff jumps with it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use. This lack of training and familiarity with the suit directly caused his inability to achieve an effective tracking position and forward drive during the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. This acute physical exhaustion from sleep deprivation and prolonged driving degraded his physical and cognitive capacity, impairing his ability to execute the demanding body position required to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove through the night without sleep and was visibly exhausted. This fatigue degraded his judgment and decision-making capacity, contributing to his decision to proceed with an unfamiliar tracking suit on a demanding cliff despite inadequate experience with the equipment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper dismissed a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' This indicates he overestimated his ability to fly the tracking suit based on watching videos rather than actual experience, misjudging his capability relative to the task demands.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight to reach the site, creating psychological investment in completing the jump. This sunk-cost commitment likely contributed to his reluctance to accept the mentor's suggestion to choose a more forgiving exit, pressing forward despite inadequate preparation with the tracking suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment familiarization before jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper purchased the tracking suit only a month prior and completed only two skydives in it, never jumping it from a cliff. He failed to adequately familiarize himself with the suit's flight characteristics through sufficient skydiving practice before attempting to use it on a slider-up cliff requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening facing the building and attempted a brake turn correction by pulling only the right toggle hard, which drove her into a diving turn into the building. Accepted practice for off-heading corrections prioritizes rear riser input (both risers to halt forward speed, then release one) rather than a single aggressive toggle input, which increases forward speed in the turn direction. The single-toggle diving turn into the building constitutes a skill-based error in off-heading correction technique.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a 180° off-heading opening facing the building, the jumper grabbed only the right toggle and pulled it hard. Accepted practice for a 180° off-heading correction is to use rear risers — both risers to halt forward speed, then release one — or to grab both toggles simultaneously and apply asymmetric input. Pulling a single toggle hard from a slider-down deep brake setting created a diving turn directly into the building rather than arresting forward movement first. The incorrect single-toggle technique produced the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient training in off-heading correction techniques
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to a 180° off-heading — grabbing a single toggle and pulling hard rather than using rear risers or both toggles to first arrest forward speed — indicates insufficient training and practice in off-heading correction procedures. Accepted practice requires extensive drilling of heading corrections at simple objects (spans) so that the correct response becomes automatic muscle memory. The jumper's technique error suggests this critical skill had not been adequately developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Upon opening with a 180° off-heading facing the building, the jumper went straight for her toggles and, finding only the right one, pulled it hard without transitioning to rear risers or attempting to locate the left toggle. This suggests channelized attention on the first control input found rather than assessing the full situation and selecting the correct correction technique. The fixation on the single available toggle excluded consideration of alternative and more appropriate responses such as rear riser input.

L0_description: The jumper chose to exit in essentially zero visibility conditions where the wall, fjord, and landing area were not visible, while all other jumpers declined to jump. This decision to jump in conditions that prevented visual reference for tracking, altitude awareness, and obstacle avoidance constitutes a clear human error. The jumper tracked into an unseen protruding ledge and impacted in terminal freefall with pilot chute still stowed, directly resulting from the decision to jump in fog that obscured known terrain hazards.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to exit in essentially zero visibility conditions when the wall, fjord, and landing area were not visible, and all other jumpers chose to hike down. He believed he could navigate by feel and clear terrain without visual reference. This decision to jump in conditions that made safe flight impossible was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper tracked into a protruding ledge approximately 120 meters below the exit, indicating his tracking line failed to clear known terrain features. His pilot chute was still stowed at impact, meaning he never initiated deployment during approximately 5 seconds of freefall. Without visual reference, he was unable to detect the terrain proximity and could not correct his flight path or deploy in time.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 10 BASE seasons and stated he had done this exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel.' This high familiarity with the site produced an unjustified reduction in alertness to the actual hazards present, leading him to believe he could safely navigate without visual reference while all other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he could track on the right bearing and clear everything by feel alone, despite zero visibility. This represents an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability to navigate terrain without visual reference. His self-assessment was inconsistent with the actual demands of the task — no amount of experience enables safe terrain clearance when protruding features are invisible.

L2_preconditions.label: Zero visibility fog during freefall
L2_preconditions.description: The fog that obscured the wall, fjord, and landing area directly degraded the jumper's ability to perceive terrain proximity during freefall and tracking. Without visual reference, he could not detect the protruding ledge, could not correct his flight path, and could not recognize the need to deploy. The fog was the environmental condition that made the L1 technique error (tracking into terrain with PC still stowed) unrecoverable. This is coded as a precondition to the 102S act, distinct from the 101D decision to jump in those conditions.

L0_description: The jumper interrupted his tracking to adjust a helmet-mounted GoPro during freefall, causing loss of tracking body position and separation from the wall. This distraction from a critical flight task—maintaining object separation through active tracking—led to drift toward the cliff with no corrective input or deployment attempt, resulting in terminal impact. Adjusting a camera mid-flight constitutes a human error that directly caused the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Marcus stopped tracking and allowed his body position to go flat while adjusting his helmet-mounted GoPro mid-flight. He drifted toward the wall without making any corrective input despite the ledge being in clear view for several seconds. He failed to monitor terrain proximity and maintain the tracking body position necessary for object separation, resulting in impact in terminal freefall without any deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus became fixated on adjusting his helmet-mounted GoPro during flight, channeling his attention onto the camera equipment to the exclusion of monitoring terrain proximity, body position, and object separation. This narrowing of attention directly enabled the skill-based attention failure identified at Layer 1.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet-mounted GoPro served as an external stimulus that diverted Marcus's attention from the primary task of tracking away from the wall. The physical act of reaching up to adjust the camera interrupted his tracking body position and removed his focus from terrain awareness at a critical moment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus was designated as the filmer on this two-way jump. The motivation to capture video of the lead jumper likely drove his decision to adjust the camera mid-flight rather than prioritize separation and tracking. This filming role created a competing priority that contributed to the attention failure.

L0_description: The jumper had a known, recurring problem with angle of attack control that caused repeated near-miss stalls. He recognized the deficiency and identified the corrective action (returning to skydiving for stall recovery training) but continued jumping cliffs without addressing it. This constitutes a clear human error: continuing to expose himself to a known lethal risk without completing the training he himself acknowledged was necessary, directly resulting in a fatal stall and terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued proximity wingsuit BASE jumping despite knowing his angle of attack control was inadequate and having experienced at least four near-miss stalls. He explicitly acknowledged needing additional skydiving training to address the deficiency but repeatedly chose to jump cliffs each weekend instead of returning to the dropzone. This decision to continue flying near terrain with a known, unresolved skill deficit placed him in a situation where a stall at insufficient altitude was inevitable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the flight, approximately 15 seconds after exit, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to become too high, entering a stall that converted his glide path from shallow to nearly vertical. This was the same technique failure pattern he had experienced on at least four prior occasions. The loss of speed and resulting vertical flight path into terrain was the direct mechanism of death.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls, explicitly acknowledged his angle of attack control was inadequate, stated he should return to the dropzone for 50 skydives to work on stall recovery, and never did so. This sustained pattern of knowing about a serious deficiency and failing to take corrective action directly enabled his continued decision to jump cliffs with an unresolved skill deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of Deviance
L2_preconditions.description: Despite four near-miss stalls each following the same pattern, the jumper continued jumping cliffs every weekend, gradually accepting repeated near-fatal events as tolerable rather than treating them as urgent signals to stop and retrain. This progressive normalization of increasingly dangerous outcomes reinforced his decision to keep flying near terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient AoA control skill for proximity flying
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's angle of attack control was self-admittedly inadequate for the demands of wingsuit proximity flying. The same technique failure—allowing AoA to get too high, entering a stall—had occurred on at least four prior occasions, demonstrating that his skill level was insufficient for the task. This training deficit was the direct basis for the recurring stall pattern that proved fatal on this jump.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 90-degree off-heading opening on an antenna with guy wires approximately 40 feet away and made no corrective input whatsoever—no riser or toggle action—despite approximately two seconds of available reaction time. This complete failure to attempt any heading correction (freezing) constitutes a human error. Additionally, the jumper had minimal antenna experience (only two prior antenna jumps from taller, more forgiving structures), and jumping an antenna at night with limited antenna-specific experience represents a progression decision inconsistent with accepted practice.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After opening with a 90-degree off-heading pointed directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away, the jumper made no corrective input whatsoever — no toggle, no riser, arms remained at his sides. Multiple witnesses confirmed he completely froze. The jumper failed to execute any heading correction despite having approximately two seconds and the knowledge/skill to use rear risers or brakes, resulting in impact with the guy wires and a fatal fall.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses stated the jumper 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with arms remaining at his sides and no corrective input attempted. This automatic physiological freeze response triggered by the unexpected sight of guy wires directly ahead rendered him unable to initiate any heading correction within the approximately two-second window available.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited antenna experience — particularly the lack of experience with guy wires in close proximity — likely contributed to his inability to execute a trained response under the sudden stress of an off-heading opening toward nearby wires. Accepted progression norms emphasize extensive practice of off-heading corrections at forgiving objects before progressing to objects where off-headings are immediately life-threatening.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for experience level
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's two previous antenna jumps were from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. Jumping a 900-foot antenna at 800 feet — where guy wires were approximately 40 feet from the opening point — represented a significantly more constrained heading environment than he had previously experienced. The failure to adequately assess how the closer guy wire proximity changed the demands of this jump contributed to his being in a situation where a 90-degree off-heading was immediately fatal.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was conducted at night (23:30), which is consistent with illegal antenna jumping where timing is driven by avoiding detection rather than optimal conditions. The illegality and nighttime execution add stress and complication that degrade performance, and the decision to proceed at night at an unfamiliar antenna type suggests motivation driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and conditions.

L0_description: The jumper knowingly used a pilot chute with a significant tear (approximately four inches of ripped mesh) despite being advised to replace it and offered spare equipment. This decision to jump with damaged equipment directly caused the PC to fail to inflate, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow / closed container impact). Using visibly damaged critical equipment constitutes a clear human error in equipment maintenance and pre-jump decision-making.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump with a pilot chute that had a large tear in the mesh, despite being advised to replace it and offered spare PCs by other jumpers. He examined the damage and consciously decided the torn PC would still function adequately. This decision directly produced the fatal outcome: the damaged PC could not inflate, resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper examined the torn PC, was advised by multiple people to replace it, was offered spare PCs, and still concluded it would 'catch enough air.' This demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of the damaged equipment's capability despite clear evidence and peer feedback to the contrary, enabling the decision to jump with known-defective gear.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes multiple human errors contributing to the fatality: the group failed to establish a proper separation plan, exit order delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the multi-way jump. The deceased tracked directly over another jumper and deployed into that jumper's inflating canopy without checking airspace, resulting in a fatal canopy entanglement. These failures in multi-way planning and airspace awareness constitute clear human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David failed to check his airspace before deploying his pilot chute, despite tracking directly over the second jumper. The information about the second jumper's position and inflating canopy was available but David did not attend to it. He pitched directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy, resulting in canopy entanglement and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way briefing and separation plan
L2_preconditions.description: The group did not establish exit order separation delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. This lack of a coordinated separation plan created the conditions under which David tracked directly over the second jumper without adequate vertical or horizontal separation, making his failure to check airspace before deployment consequential.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: David failed to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers in his airspace during a multi-way jump. Despite tracking directly over the second jumper, he did not check below before deploying, indicating a failure to maintain the attention required for safe deployment in proximity to other jumpers.

L0_description: The jumper deliberately delayed pilot chute extraction too long, pulling very low with insufficient altitude for canopy inflation. The narrative explicitly states he had a pattern of progressively pushing delays longer despite warnings from other jumpers, and on this jump he 'simply pushed it one second too far.' This constitutes a clear human error in judgment and action.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to deploy at a dangerously low altitude, continuing a pattern of progressively extending delays from 4 seconds to 8+ seconds over the preceding season. On this jump he pulled just above the water, leaving insufficient altitude for the canopy to complete extraction and inflation before impact. The narrative explicitly states there was no gear issue, no wind issue, and no unstable exit — the decision to pull at that altitude was the direct producing mechanism of the fatality.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of his progressively lower pulls through feedback from other jumpers who spoke to him about it multiple times. Despite this explicit external warning, he failed to take corrective action and continued extending his delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the preceding season. This sustained pattern of inaction in the face of known risk directly enabled the fatal decision to pull one second too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's progressive extension of delays from 4 to 8+ seconds over a single season, despite repeated warnings from peers, indicates he unreasonably overestimated his ability to judge minimum deployment altitude. Each successful low pull reinforced his belief that he could push further, creating a self-assessment inconsistent with the actual margin available on a span jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's motivation to progressively extend delays was driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and safe margins. The pattern of pushing delays longer and longer suggests a performance-driven or thrill-seeking motivation that overrode prudent judgment about minimum safe deployment altitude.

L0_description: The jumper flew an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it before using it for BASE, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 wingsuit skydives with the specific suit. This premature upgrade to a high-performance suit he had not mastered led to a stall and unrecoverable loss of glide during proximity flight, constituting human error in equipment selection and progression.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the wingsuit flight, the jumper's angle of attack shifted to a position that stalled the suit, causing his glide to transition from shallow to nearly vertical. He was unable to recover from the stall, sinking rapidly until impact. The execution failure was in managing AoA during flight — he flew too slowly or too head-high, entering a stall regime from which he could not recover.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) off a cliff after only approximately 10 skydives in the suit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. The narrative explicitly states the suit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand, and he had not put in that time. This decision to use an insufficiently familiar expert suit for proximity terrain flying directly set up the conditions for the fatal stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit-specific experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. Expert-level wingsuits have stall boundaries that require many flights to understand. His insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight envelope directly contributed to his inability to manage AoA and recover from the stall during flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Premature progression to expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 40 wingsuit BASE jumps using intermediate suits and only about 10 skydives in the Aura 1, the jumper's overall experience level was insufficient for an expert-level wingsuit in BASE. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives including 40-60 with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE. His 200 total wingsuit skydives met the overall minimum, but his near-total lack of time in the expert suit meant he lacked the skill foundation needed to make a sound judgment about using it off a cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to fly an expert-level wingsuit off a cliff after only ~10 skydives in it, indicating he overestimated his ability to safely manage the suit's flight characteristics in a BASE environment. The narrative explicitly states the suit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand and that he had not put in that time, yet he proceeded to fly a proximity line, demonstrating a misjudgment of his capability relative to the task demands.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity during flight
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was described as sunny and hot with thermals producing. Thermal activity can create unpredictable lift and sink zones along a flight path, potentially contributing to the AoA shift and stall by altering the air mass the jumper was flying through. While the primary cause of the stall was the jumper's technique, active thermals during a hot afternoon in Chamonix could have degraded his ability to maintain stable flight, particularly given his unfamiliarity with the suit's stall boundaries.

L0_description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and 0 BASE jumps, which is drastically below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE jumping. This constitutes a 'death camper' scenario. Additionally, the jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump with zero BASE experience and zero wingsuit/tracking suit BASE experience, representing an extreme violation of accepted progression norms. The narrative indicates possible unstable exit, insufficient forward separation, and excessive delay, all pointing to human error rooted in inadequate skill and experience for the attempted jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit BASE jump from a cliff with only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, massively below the accepted minimums of 200+ skydives and proper BASE progression. This represents a 'death camper' scenario where the jumper lacked any meaningful freefall skills, canopy skills, tracking ability, or BASE-specific training. The decision to attempt this jump was itself sufficient to produce the fatal outcome regardless of execution quality, as the jumper had no foundation of skills to draw upon.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an unstable exit and failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff. With essentially no freefall experience, the jumper lacked the body control skills needed to maintain a stable exit, establish an effective sub-terminal track, and deploy at an appropriate altitude with sufficient object clearance. The result was impact with terrain, with the canopy found out but the jumper having sustained fatal head injuries consistent with cliff strike or ground impact near the exit point.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient training and experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives, zero BASE jumps, zero wingsuit skydives, and no reported BASE training or mentorship. This is massively below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, and represents a complete absence of the foundational skills (freefall body control, canopy piloting, tracking) needed to evaluate whether a slider-up cliff jump was within their capability. This lack of experience directly enabled the catastrophically poor decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: No freefall or tracking competence
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives total and no BASE jumps, the jumper had no meaningful freefall body position skills, no sub-terminal tracking ability, and no experience with BASE deployment sequences. Accepted progression requires extensive skydiving, tracking suit proficiency built over 40-50+ tracking suit skydives, and progression through simple BASE objects before attempting slider-up cliffs. The complete absence of these skills made it impossible for the jumper to execute a stable exit, effective track, or properly timed deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump with 5 skydives and zero BASE experience, indicating a gross overestimation of their own capability relative to the demands of the task. The gap between the jumper's actual ability and the minimum competence required for this jump type is so extreme that proceeding implies the jumper fundamentally misjudged what was required or believed they could perform far beyond their actual skill level.

L0_description: The jumper made a critical packing error by placing the pilot chute inside the main container beneath the closing flaps instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. This resulted in a total malfunction (PC in tow / PC trapped in container) when the BOC handle extracted without the PC. Additionally, the jumper chose to jump in deteriorating weather with increasing winds and rain, which constitutes a decision to jump in conditions that were observable and unfavorable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container on top of the folded canopy instead of routing it out to the BOC pouch. This was a procedural omission in a task the jumper knew how to perform — the PC must be routed to the BOC pocket, not stowed inside the container. When the jumper pulled the BOC handle in freefall, it extracted cleanly but no pilot chute was attached, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down due to deteriorating weather. This indecision and the stress of the weather situation likely diverted mental attention away from the packing task, contributing to the critical omission of failing to route the PC to the BOC pouch. The error — placing the PC inside the container rather than routing it out — is a fundamental procedural step that a jumper with 90 BASE jumps would normally perform correctly, suggesting degraded attention during packing rather than a knowledge deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes deteriorating weather with increasing wind and rain, and the jumper going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down. Being the last one at the exit while conditions worsened creates pressure to either commit quickly or abandon. This pressure to resolve the decision — combined with worsening conditions that may have rushed the packing process — contributed to the procedural omission during packing.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check or trace of the deployment system from BOC handle through bridle to PC would have revealed that the PC was not in the BOC pouch but was instead trapped inside the container. The failure to perform this verification before jumping allowed the packing error to go undetected and directly enabled the total malfunction.

L0_description: The narrative is entirely absent and no structured fields provide meaningful information beyond location, date, and object type. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a non-human cause.

The narrative provides minimal information: the jumper impacted talus below the exit with the canopy out, and there were no witnesses. No details are available regarding experience, equipment configuration, packing, weather, body position, or the sequence of events leading to impact. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a specific non-human cause.

Two experienced witnesses provide contradictory accounts—one indicating a late pull and the other indicating a pilot chute in tow malfunction with a timely deployment. The gear was not available for inspection, making it impossible to determine whether the cause was a human error (late deployment) or an equipment-related issue. The narrative does not contain sufficient information to support either 001HE or 002OC.

The jumper maintained an active body position throughout freefall but never attempted deployment, with no equipment malfunction, no medical event, and no toxicological findings to explain the failure to pull. The narrative explicitly states 'nobody knows why he did not pull.' There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a positive non-human cause.

The narrative is a tribute and contains no technical details about the accident. The cause of death is impact in terminal freefall during proximity flying, but the narrative provides no information about what went wrong — whether it was a flight error, environmental factor, equipment issue, or other cause. Without any factual account of the jump, neither human error nor other cause can be supported.

The jumper was found dead with canopy deployed after jumping alone with no witnesses. All proposed explanations (off-heading, packing issue, thermal conditions) are explicitly described as speculation. No one witnessed the jump and no gear inspection was conducted before removal, leaving insufficient information to determine whether human error or another cause led to the fatality.

The jumper went missing and was found months later at the base of a cliff. No witnesses observed the jump, the date and weather conditions are unknown, and no evidence exists to determine what happened. While the site is known for sudden unpredictable crosswinds, there is no positive evidence establishing whether human error or an unforeseeable environmental event caused the fatality. The narrative explicitly states it is impossible to determine what happened.

The narrative provides virtually no information about the jump, the jumper, the equipment, or the circumstances of the fatality. The body was recovered but no details on gear, experience, weather, or sequence of events are available. There is insufficient information to deduce either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The jumper pulled too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. Regardless of the debated proximate cause (sun glare, misjudging descent rate in a tracking suit, or attention fixation), all proposed explanations involve human error in altitude awareness and deployment timing. The jumper failed to deploy at a survivable altitude, which constitutes a human error in the deployment decision.

The narrative states the cause of death was 'Malfunction, Misrigging' and explicitly rules out manufacturer error ('It was clear that it is not a manufacturer error'). This indicates the malfunction resulted from the jumper's own rigging or packing errors, which constitutes human error in equipment preparation. The jumper also failed to resolve the malfunction during 8 seconds of attempted correction, suggesting possible inadequate malfunction response, though the specific canopy state could not be determined from the video.
